---
title: "Linear models with a single continuous predictor - Study Notes"
author: "Michelle Pinard & Deon Roos"
date: 'Last updated: `r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  word_document:
    toc: yes
    reference_docx: "../sbstemplate.docx"
---

# Purpose

Model how a continuous response variable changes with a single continuous explanatory variable. The question is framed as a straight-line trend and the goal is to obtain interpretable parameters and a clear figure showing the fitted relationship.

# How to read the model

A linear model with one continuous predictor estimates an intercept and a slope. The intercept ($\beta_0$) is the expected outcome when the explanatory variable equals zero. The slope ($\beta_1$) is the expected change in the response for a one-unit increase in the explanatory variable. These parameters define the "line of best fit," which balances positive and negative residuals

# How the code is written and relates to the model

If the `R` code is `lm(y ~ x, data = my_data)`, this maps to:

$y_i \sim Normal(\mu_i, \sigma)$

$\mu_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times x_i$

# Interpreting output

-   Intercept ($\beta_0$): expected response when $x = 0$ (pay attention to if $x = 0$ is biologically meaningful in your context, e.g. can a minke whale weigh 0 kg?)

-   Slope ($\beta_1$): expected change in response per unit $x$.

-   Summarise parameter estimates to an appropriate level of precision (e.g. to three decimal places or whatever makes sense in the given context).

# Workflow carried across

Use the same sequence as always: formulate the question, do quick EDA, note hidden assumptions, fit the model, summarise, and produce a clear plot of the fitted relationship. Diagnostic checking is in the next Study Note.

# Useful code examples

```{r, eval = F}
# Fit model
m <- lm(y ~ x, data = d)

# Summary of model
summary(m)

# Create a simple prediction to show relationship
fake <- data.frame(x = seq(min(d$x), max(d$x), length.out = 25))
pr   <- predict(m, newdata = fake, se.fit = TRUE)

fake$fit = pr$fit

# Plot the relationship
ggplot(fake, aes(x, fit)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Explanatory", y = "Predicted mean")

# Quick way to plot
library(ggeffects)
plot(ggpredict(m))
```
